plainMeaning: The hymn keeps bowing to one holy town after another — Bhinmal and Koṭaḍā, the Bāhaḍamer (Barmer) region, and lovely Rāyadhaṇapur (Radhanpur).
recitation: bhinnamāl ne koṭaḍe, sā॰ ॥ bāhaḍamer majhār | rāyadhaṇapur raḷiyāmaṇũ, sā॰

wordByWord.gloss: Bhinmal (ancient Śrīmāla), the Jina-temple town in Jālor, Rajasthan
wordByWord.etymology: Place-name from Śrīmāla → Bhillamāla → Bhinmāl; the old Gurjara capital and famed Śrīmālī-Jain seat (Pārśvanātha Bahuttarī Jinālaya).
wordByWord.notes: A named earthly tīrtha (aśāśvata caitya), not a cosmic shrine.

wordByWord.etymology: Gujarati copulative conjunction 'ne' (and), linking the two town-names.
koṭ (fort, from Skt. koṭṭa) + diminutive -ḍā → Koṭaḍā; locative -e ('at Koṭaḍā').
wordByWord.notes: Several Marwar towns bear this name; precise site uncertain (see notes).

wordByWord.etymology: The circle ૦ marks an abbreviation; 'sā' opens the recurring burden-line repeated after each town-cluster.
wordByWord.notes: The book prints only the truncated cue; the full refrain word is not expanded here (uncertain).

wordByWord.gloss: [double daṇḍa — caesura before the refrain/second quarter]
wordByWord.etymology: Scribal/metrical punctuation marking a pause within the dhāla line.
Traditionally 'Bāhaḍa-meru' — the hill/seat founded by Bāhaḍa (Vāgbhaṭa); later Bāhaḍmer → Bārmer.
Old Gujarati/Marwari poetic locative from Skt. madhya → Pkt. majjha → majhār ('in the middle of').
wordByWord.notes: Functions as a postposition gathering the surrounding district under Bāhaḍamer.

wordByWord.etymology: rāya (Skt. rāja, king) + dhaṇa (Skt. dhana, wealth) + pur (city) — 'the king's wealth-city'; modern Rādhanpur.
wordByWord.notes: Identification with Radhanpur (Patan dist., Gujarat) is contextually secure (see notes).

wordByWord.etymology: Gujarati raḷiyāmaṇũ, from raḷiyāt/raḷvũ (joy, to delight) + qualitative suffix -maṇũ — 'that which gives delight'.
wordByWord.notes: The single evaluative epithet of the verse, lavished on Rāyadhaṇapur.

wordByWord.etymology: Same truncated burden-cue closing the second line as it did the first.
wordByWord.notes: Repetition of the dhāla refrain marker.

literalTranslation: Bhinmal and at Koṭaḍā, [refrain] — within Bāhaḍamer (Barmer); the lovely Rāyadhaṇapur (Radhanpur), [refrain].
idiomaticTranslation: I bow at Bhinmal and at Koṭaḍā — and there, set in the heart of the Bāhaḍamer (Barmer) country, and at Rāyadhaṇapur, that lovely town: to the Jina enshrined in each, reverence.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This couplet is a single bead in the dhāla-8 tīrthamālā, and its whole work is to keep the reciter's inner gaze moving across the map of living Jaina shrines. Where the previous verse swept the northern Marwar arc — Bikaner, Medta, Sirohi, Ābu, Ranakpur, Sadri — this verse steps south and west: Bhinmal (the ancient Śrīmāla, mother-town of the Śrīmālī Jains), neighbouring Koṭaḍā, the Bāhaḍamer (Barmer) tract, and Rāyadhaṇapur on the threshold of Gujarat. The geography is not random; it traces a pilgrim's actual road, so that the following verse can land in core Gujarat (Sānchor, Jālor, Godī-Pārśva, Pāṭaṇ, Amdāvād). Two small words carry the poetry. 'Majhār' ('in the midst of') does more than fill the metre — it folds an entire district into one bow, telling the reciter that not just the named town but the temples scattered around Bāhaḍamer are being saluted. And 'raḷiyāmaṇũ' ('lovely') is the verse's only adjective: amid a bare roll-call of names the poet pauses to call Rāyadhaṇapur beautiful, modelling the affectionate gaze a devotee should turn on every tīrtha. The clipped refrain 'sā॰' after each half keeps the chant's pulse, binding this couplet to its neighbours in one continuous garland.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse enacts bhāva-yātrā — pilgrimage performed in the mind. Each place-name is shorthand for the Jina-image enshrined there, so naming the town is itself a caitya-vandana, a bow to that pratimā. Doctrinally these are aśāśvata (made-in-time) earthly caityas, distinct from the śāśvata cosmic shrines counted in the earlier dhālas; reverencing them with feeling nourishes samyaktva (right faith) and accrues puṇya without a single step taken. The lavishing of 'raḷiyāmaṇũ' on one town teaches that bhāva — warm devotional attention — not distance travelled, is what makes a vandana fruitful.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: While chanting, the reciter pictures the Jina-temple at each named town and offers an inward bow, turning a list of unfamiliar place-names into a felt morning pilgrimage. The word 'majhār' is a cue to include the lesser shrines around Barmer, and 'raḷiyāmaṇũ' a cue to let real affection, not rote, color the bow.

crossReferences.text: Aṣṭottarī Tīrthamālā (108 tīrthas) — the Pakkhī tīrtha-litany parallel to this dhāla-8 garland
Bhinmal as Śrīmāla and the Bahuttarī (72) Jinālaya of Pārśvanātha

sources.title: Bahuter Jinalya (Bhinmal) — Jainsite
sources.consultedFor: Bhinmal as a living Jaina tīrtha (Pārśvanātha Bahuttarī Jinālaya), supporting its place in the tīrthamālā.

sources.title: Barmer — Wikipedia
sources.consultedFor: Identification of Bāhaḍamer with modern Barmer and its 'Bāhaḍa' foundation tradition.

sources.title: Radhanpur — Wikipedia
sources.consultedFor: Identification of Rāyadhaṇapur with Radhanpur on the Gujarat–Marwar border.

sources.title: मध्य — Wiktionary
sources.consultedFor: Etymology of 'majhār' via Skt. madhya → Pkt. majjha ('in the midst of').

notes: The refrain 'સા૦ (sā॰)' is printed only as an abbreviation (circle ૦); the book does not expand its full burden-word here, so its sense is left as an unexpanded refrain marker (uncertain). 'કોટડે (Koṭaḍā)' is a common Marwar fort-town name; the exact site near Bhinmal/Barmer is not securely fixed. 'રાયધણપુર' is read as Radhanpur (Patan dist., Gujarat) on geographic grounds — it sits between the Marwar towns of this verse and the Gujarat towns of the next; the older form may also be glossed 'Rāidhanpur'. 'મઝાર (majhār)' is taken as the Old Gujarati/Marwari poetic locative 'within/in the midst of' (< madhya), not the Arabic mazār ('tomb-shrine'). Spelling, daṇḍas (॥ ।) and the abbreviation circle are kept verbatim per the book's orthography.
